channel.title: SecHunter — Bug Bounty & Security Research
channel.description: Recent content on SecHunter — Bug Bounty & Security Research

item.title: Advanced Tools — Deep Dive Day 168
item.description:
Advanced Tools — Deep Dive Day 168

In the rapidly evolving landscape of cybersecurity, staying ahead of threats requires more than just awareness — it demands mastery of the right tools. Welcome to Advanced Tools — Deep Dive Day 168, where we explore the cutting-edge arsenal that every security professional, penetration tester, and bug bounty hunter needs in their toolkit. With over 144 npm packages recently compromised through hijacked contributor accounts and CISA warnings about actively exploited JCE flaws, the threat landscape has never been more dynamic. This deep dive will equip you with the knowledge to leverage advanced security tools effectively in your daily operations.

item.title: About SecHunter
item.description:
About SecHunter

I’m a security researcher and bug bounty hunter. I find vulnerabilities in web applications, APIs, and open source software — and get paid for it.

What I Do

  • Bug Bounty Hunting — Finding vulnerabilities on HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Intigriti
  • Penetration Testing — Testing company security (authorized, of course)
  • Security Research — Finding new vulnerabilities in popular software
  • Content Creation — Sharing knowledge through blog posts and tutorials

Skills

  • Web Application Security (OWASP Top 10)
  • API Security Testing (REST, GraphQL)
  • IDOR, XSS, SSRF, SQLi, Authentication Bypass
  • Open Source Code Review
  • Penetration Testing
  • Security Automation (Python, Bash)

Platforms

  • HackerOne
  • Bugcrowd
  • GitHub
  • Twitter

Contact

Email: sechunter@protonmail.com

item.title: How I Found My First $500 Bug — IDOR on a Popular Platform
item.description:
How I Found My First $500 Bug

Everyone remembers their first bounty. Here’s how I found an IDOR vulnerability that earned me $500 on my first serious hunting attempt.

The Target

A popular SaaS platform with a bug bounty program on HackerOne. The program had a $500 minimum bounty for medium-severity findings.

The Recon

I started by creating two accounts:

  • Account A (attacker): testuser1@mailinator.com
  • Account B (victim): testuser2@mailinator.com

I mapped the application by browsing every feature:

item.title: How to Start Bug Bounty Hunting in 2026 — Complete Beginner Guide
item.description:
How to Start Bug Bounty Hunting in 2026

Bug bounty hunting is one of the best ways to earn money using security skills. Companies pay you to find vulnerabilities in their software. No degree required. No experience needed. Just skills and persistence.

What is Bug Bounty?

Bug bounty programs are deals offered by companies where they pay security researchers for finding and reporting vulnerabilities. You find a bug, report it, and get paid.

item.title: IDOR Hunting Methodology — How to Find Insecure Direct Object References
item.description:
IDOR Hunting Methodology

IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) is the #1 most rewarded vulnerability class in bug bounty programs. It’s simple to find, easy to explain, and has high impact.

What is IDOR?

IDOR occurs when an application uses user-supplied input to directly access an object without verifying that the user is authorized to access it.

Example:

# Your own profile
GET /api/users/1234/profile → 200 OK (your data)

# Attacker changes ID
GET /api/users/1235/profile → 200 OK (someone else's data!)

Why IDOR Pays So Well

  • High impact: Access to other users’ private data
  • Easy to demonstrate: Just change a number
  • Consistent payouts: $500-$25,000 across platforms
  • High frequency: Found in almost every web application

Types of IDOR

1. Horizontal IDOR

Access another user’s data at the same privilege level.

item.title: Top 10 Bug Bounty Tools You Need in 2026
item.description:
Top 10 Bug Bounty Tools for 2026

Every hunter needs a solid toolkit. Here are the 10 essential tools for bug bounty hunting — all free and open source.

1. curl — HTTP Swiss Army Knife

What: Command-line HTTP client Use: API testing, sending custom requests, debugging Why: Available on every system, scriptable, supports all HTTP methods

# Basic request
curl https://target.com/api/users/1

# With headers
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer ***" \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 https://target.com/api/users/1

# POST request
curl -X POST -d '{"name":"test"}' \
 https://target.com/api/users

2. nmap — Network Scanner

What: Port scanner and service detector Use: Finding open ports, identifying services Why: Essential for recon, finds attack surface

365-Day Cybersecurity Blog Content Calendar

Complete Coverage of Every Cybersecurity Topic

JANUARY — Foundations & Networking

Week 1: Introduction to Cybersecurity

  • Day 1: What is Cybersecurity? Complete Beginner Guide
  • Day 2: History of Cybersecurity — From Creeper to Modern Threats
  • Day 3: Types of Cybersecurity — Network, Application, Cloud, IoT
  • Day 4: CIA Triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
  • Day 5: Cybersecurity Frameworks — NIST, ISO 27001, CIS Controls
  • Day 6: Cybersecurity Roles — SOC Analyst, Pentester, CISO
  • Day 7: How to Start a Career in Cybersecurity

Week 2: Networking Fundamentals

  • Day 8: OSI Model Explained — All 7 Layers
  • Day 9: TCP/IP Protocol Suite — Complete Guide
  • Day 10: IP Addressing, Subnetting, and CIDR
  • Day 11: DNS — How Domain Name System Works
  • Day 12: HTTP vs HTTPS — Web Protocols Explained
  • Day 13: Firewalls, Routers, and Switches
  • Day 14: VPNs — How Virtual Private Networks Work

Week 3: Operating Systems Security

  • Day 15: Linux Security Fundamentals
  • Day 16: Windows Security Architecture
  • Day 17: macOS Security Features
  • Day 18: File Permissions and Access Control
  • Day 19: User Management and Privilege Escalation
  • Day 20: Process Management and Monitoring
  • Day 21: Log Analysis and Auditing

Week 4: Cryptography Basics

  • Day 22: What is Cryptography? Complete Guide
  • Day 23: Symmetric Encryption — AES, DES, 3DES
  • Day 24: Asymmetric Encryption — RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman
  • Day 25: Hashing — SHA-256, MD5, bcrypt
  • Day 26: Digital Signatures and Certificates
  • Day 27: PKI — Public Key Infrastructure
  • Day 28: TLS/SSL — How HTTPS Works

FEBRUARY — Web Application Security

Week 1: Web Fundamentals

  • Day 29: How the Web Works — Complete Architecture
  • Day 30: HTML, CSS, JavaScript Security
  • Day 31: Cookies, Sessions, and Tokens
  • Day 32: CORS — Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
  • Day 33: Content Security Policy (CSP)
  • Day 34: Subresource Integrity (SRI)
  • Day 35: SameSite Cookies and Security Headers

Week 2: OWASP Top 10 — Part 1

  • Day 36: OWASP Top 10 Overview — 2023 Edition
  • Day 37: A01 — Broken Access Control (Deep Dive)
  • Day 38: A02 — Cryptographic Failures
  • Day 39: A03 — Injection Attacks (SQLi, NoSQLi, LDAPi)
  • Day 40: A04 — Insecure Design
  • Day 41: A05 — Security Misconfiguration
  • Day 42: A06 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

Week 3: OWASP Top 10 — Part 2

  • Day 43: A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures
  • Day 44: A08 — Software and Data Integrity Failures
  • Day 45: A09 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
  • Day 46: A10 — Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
  • Day 47: Beyond OWASP — Additional Web Vulnerabilities
  • Day 48: Web Security Testing Methodology
  • Day 49: Web Application Firewall (WAF) Bypass Techniques

Week 4: API Security

  • Day 50: REST API Security Best Practices
  • Day 51: GraphQL Security — Common Vulnerabilities
  • Day 52: OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect Security
  • Day 53: JWT Security — Attacks and Defenses
  • Day 54: API Rate Limiting and Throttling
  • Day 55: API Authentication Patterns
  • Day 56: API Security Testing Tools and Techniques

MARCH — Penetration Testing

Week 1: Recon & Scanning

  • Day 57: Penetration Testing Methodology — PTES, OWASP, NIST
  • Day 58: Passive Reconnaissance Techniques
  • Day 59: Active Reconnaissance and Scanning
  • Day 60: Subdomain Enumeration — Complete Guide
  • Day 61: Port Scanning with Nmap — Advanced Techniques
  • Day 62: Service Fingerprinting and Version Detection
  • Day 63: OSINT — Open Source Intelligence Gathering

Week 2: Vulnerability Assessment

  • Day 64: Vulnerability Scanning with Nessus
  • Day 65: Web Vulnerability Scanning with Nikto
  • Day 66: Automated vs Manual Testing
  • Day 67: Vulnerability Classification and Scoring (CVSS)
  • Day 68: False Positive Management
  • Day 69: Vulnerability Assessment Reports
  • Day 70: Risk Assessment Methodology

Week 3: Exploitation

  • Day 71: Exploitation Fundamentals
  • Day 72: Metasploit Framework — Complete Guide
  • Day 73: Manual Exploitation Techniques
  • Day 74: Password Attacks — Cracking and Brute Force
  • Day 75: Client-Side Attacks
  • Day 76: Post-Exploitation Techniques
  • Day 77: Privilege Escalation — Linux and Windows

Week 4: Post-Exploitation & Reporting

  • Day 78: Lateral Movement Techniques

  • Day 80: Persistence Mechanisms
  • Day 81: Anti-Forensics and Evasion
  • Day 82: Writing Penetration Test Reports
  • Day 83: Executive Summary Writing
  • Day 84: Remediation Recommendations

APRIL — Network Security

Week 1: Network Defense

  • Day 85: Network Security Architecture
  • Day 86: Firewall Configuration and Management

Week 2: Wireless Security

Week 3: Cloud Security

  • Day 99: Cloud Computing Security Overview

Week 4: Email Security

MAY — Malware & Threats

Week 1: Malware Analysis

Week 2: Advanced Threats

  • Day 120: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)

  • Day 124: Fileless Malware

Week 3: Social Engineering

Week 4: DDoS and Botnets

  • Day 136: Amplification Attacks
  • Day 137: Application Layer DDoS
  • Day 138: DDoS Mitigation Services

JUNE — Bug Bounty & Ethical Hacking

Week 1: Bug Bounty Fundamentals

  • Day 141: What is Bug Bounty? Complete Guide
  • Day 142: Bug Bounty Platforms — HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Intigriti
  • Day 143: How to Choose Your First Target
  • Day 144: Bug Bounty Methodology

  • Day 147: Building Your Bug Bounty Reputation

Week 2: Vulnerability Classes — Part 1

  • Day 150: CSRF — Cross-Site Request Forgery
  • Day 151: SQL Injection — Complete Guide
  • Day 152: NoSQL Injection
  • Day 153: Command Injection
  • Day 154: LDAP Injection

Week 3: Vulnerability Classes — Part 2

  • Day 157: Path Traversal
  • Day 158: Open Redirect
  • Day 159: Authentication Bypass
  • Day 160: Privilege Escalation
  • Day 161: Business Logic Vulnerabilities

Week 4: Advanced Bug Bounty

  • Day 165: Insecure Deserialization
  • Day 166: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
  • Day 167: Prototype Pollution

JULY — AI & Machine Learning Security

Week 1: AI Security Fundamentals

  • Day 169: Introduction to AI Security

Week 2: AI Attacks

Week 4: AI Tools for Security

Week 3: Advanced Web Exploitation

Week 4: Red Team Operations
